context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to write my reflection on the neoliberal vision of the state vs. the role of the state in the East Asian miracle, and I’m kind of fuzzy on the differences. Could you help me sort out the main contrast, especially where civil society/labor/democracy fits in?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — let's build from what you already know. When you hear 'neoliberal vision of the state,' what comes to mind? What do you think neoliberalism says the state *should* and *should not* do in an economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad opening question that covers almost the entire prompt. Rather than mapping out all the contrasts for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them to activate prior knowledge first. I'll start with what they already know about neoliberalism before moving to the East Asian comparison. This keeps scaffolding minimal and puts the intellectual work on the student.

exchanges.student: I think neoliberalism wants a smaller state and more reliance on markets, but I’m not fully sure how far that goes. Like, does the state still set rules and protect property, but avoid directing industries or welfare stuff?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. So if the neoliberal state steps back from directing industries and welfare, what's the underlying reasoning? Why do neoliberals believe markets, rather than states, should be the primary decision-makers in an economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition about neoliberalism. They've correctly identified the core idea of market reliance and limited state intervention, and they're already self-questioning about the nuances (property rights, rule-setting). I should affirm the direction without giving more, and push them to sharpen their understanding by asking what the logic or justification behind this vision is. This will help them later when they need to contrast it with East Asia.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing the idea is that markets are supposed to be more efficient and better at responding to prices and competition, while states are seen as too political or corrupt. Is it also about the belief that state planning distorts incentives?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on all three counts. Now here's the pivot: East Asian economies like South Korea, Taiwan, and Japan grew dramatically in the postwar period — but their states were very actively involved in directing industries and the economy. How does that sit with the neoliberal logic you just described? What does that suggest about the relationship between state intervention and development there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a strong foundation on neoliberalism - they've correctly identified efficiency arguments, the skepticism of state capacity, and the incentive distortion concern. This is good enough to pivot toward the East Asian case now, which is the core contrast the assignment requires. I should affirm briefly and push them to think about East Asia, where the state visibly contradicted the neoliberal model.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get a little stuck. It seems like East Asia kind of challenges the neoliberal view, because those states didn’t just get out of the way — they picked industries, protected some sectors, and coordinated investment. So I think it suggests state intervention can actually help development, but I’m not sure how to say that without making it sound like “the state should always intervene.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a careful and smart distinction to make. So what do you think was *specific* about how East Asian states intervened? Was it just that they intervened, or was there something particular about *how* they did it that might explain why it worked?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good analytical move - they've identified the tension and are already being careful not to overclaim. This is exactly the kind of nuanced thinking the assignment requires. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should push them to think about what was distinctive about East Asian state intervention - what made it work there - so they can articulate a more precise argument. This also sets up the new institutionalist angle mentioned in the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think it wasn’t just intervention by itself — it was more selective and strategic. Maybe the state had strong bureaucratic capacity and worked closely with business, so intervention was disciplined instead of random?
exchanges.tutor: Good — that disciplined, strategic quality is really important to capture in your reflection. Now the prompt also asks specifically about civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. What do you know or suspect about how workers, unions, or democratic participation figured into that model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is zeroing in on a key concept - the developmental state - without me naming it. They've identified bureaucratic capacity and state-business coordination, which are central. I should push them now toward the civil society/labor/democracy part of the prompt, which they haven't touched yet and which is equally important for the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think this part is fuzzier for me. My impression is that a lot of the early East Asian growth cases were not very democratic at first, and labor was often controlled or suppressed so the state could keep wages competitive and avoid unrest. But I’m not sure if I’m oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor: You're not oversimplifying — that tension is real and historically documented. So what does that complicate for your argument? If the East Asian model challenges neoliberalism but also marginalized labor and democracy, what does that mean for how you evaluate it in your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's instinct is historically accurate and importantly honest about uncertainty. This is a crucial observation for the assignment - the East Asian model's relationship with labor and democracy is genuinely complicated and somewhat uncomfortable. I should affirm their direction and push them to think about what that implies for the argument they're building - specifically, what does it mean for development theory if the model that challenges neoliberalism also suppressed labor and democracy?

exchanges.student: I think it means I can’t treat the East Asian model as just a success story. It may show that state intervention can promote growth, but also that growth can depend on unequal power relations, weak labor rights, and limited democracy. I’m trying to figure out how to balance that without sounding all over the place.
exchanges.tutor: That tension is actually the most intellectually honest place to be — don't try to resolve it artificially. Now, the prompt asks you to connect all of this to new institutionalist theories. What do you know about that perspective, and how might it help make sense of what you just described about the East Asian state?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a genuinely sophisticated analytical position - they're holding the tension between the model's economic success and its political costs. This is exactly the kind of critical thinking the assignment calls for. They're also asking implicitly about structure and argumentation. Rather than giving writing advice (which I shouldn't do), I should push them toward the last part of the prompt they haven't addressed: new institutionalist theories. This will help them see how their argument connects to the bigger theoretical picture the assignment requires.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit shaky on new institutionalism. I think it’s about how institutions shape behavior and outcomes, so maybe the point is that development depends not just on “more state” or “less state,” but on what kind of institutions the state has? Could you help me make that connection more clearly?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right instinct. So apply that directly: if new institutionalism says institutions shape outcomes, what does that tell us about *why* East Asian intervention worked where other state interventions might not have? How does that reframe the 'state vs. market' debate you started with? Once you can answer that, you have the spine of your reflection — I'd encourage you to try drafting a paragraph or two pulling everything together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a correct intuition about new institutionalism. However, they're asking me to make the connection for them, which I shouldn't do - that's their intellectual work. I should affirm their direction and push them to make the connection themselves by asking a targeted question. We're also getting close to the point where they should start integrating everything into a written attempt, given we've covered all major parts of the prompt across several exchanges.

exchanges.student: I think it reframes it as not a simple state-versus-market argument. What seems important is whether state institutions are capable, coordinated, and embedded enough to guide markets effectively, so East Asian cases suggest intervention works under certain institutional conditions rather than automatically. I’m still unsure how to phrase the civil society/labor part in the same paragraph without it feeling tacked on.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: if institutions shape outcomes, are labor suppression and limited democracy *incidental* to the East Asian model, or are they part of the institutional conditions you just described? How does your answer to that change the argument? That's your connection — now try writing a full draft and see how it comes together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the major components of the prompt with good analytical depth. They're asking for writing help on how to integrate the civil society/labor point, but I shouldn't give language or structural suggestions - that's their writing work. What I can do is push them to think about how the civil society/labor point *logically* connects to the institutional argument they just made, so they can find the integration themselves. We've had a very productive conversation and this is a good moment to push them toward a full draft.

exchanges.student: Oh, okay — then I think labor control and limited democracy might not just be side details, but part of the institutional setup that made that model possible, which makes the model more ethically and politically complicated. I’m going to try drafting it that way, but I may need help tightening the thesis after I get something down.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong analytical foundation — you've worked through all the major parts of the prompt thoughtfully. One reminder: when you share your draft, I won't edit or polish it for you, but I can ask questions that help *you* see where the argument might need more work. Go ahead and draft — looking forward to seeing what you put together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has had a productive journey and arrived at a genuinely sophisticated analytical position covering all parts of the prompt. They're ready to draft. I should affirm their progress, remind them that I can't edit or revise their writing, but that I'm happy to ask questions about their draft once they share it. This sets clear expectations for the next interaction.
